title: Capitalism and Contested Publicity: A Conversation with Nancy Fraser
author: Kempf and Sevignani

opening_move.description: The paper opens by framing the current crisis of the bourgeois public sphere: subaltern counterpublics have been theorized by Fraser as potentially transformative, but now right-wing counterpublics and capitalist digital media complicate this optimistic picture. The interview is positioned as continuing a workshop discussion with Fraser in June 2022.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Can a socialist counter-hegemony be articulated through the public sphere when it is dominated by capitalist logics? Fraser's analysis ties public sphere theory to her theory of capitalism.

theoretical_positioning.description: The interview format means the theoretical positioning is Fraser's own: she distinguishes subaltern counterpublics from right-wing counterpublics, argues against left populism (Laclau's 'empty signifier') in favour of class-based counter-hegemony, links public sphere to capitalism theory, and argues for 'boundary struggles' to politicize the private. Kempf and Sevignani provide framing and questions.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Interview format; reference to contemporary phenomena (social media, Trump, Cambridge Analytica, COVID-era politics) as illustrative material.
data_sources: contemporary examples: Trump election, social media fragmentation
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conversational elaboration of theoretical positions; Fraser refines her concepts of subaltern counterpublics, public-at-large, and capitalism in light of interviewers' questions.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is a genre-hybrid: a theorist-interview with framing essay. The epistemic mode is primarily normative — Fraser elaborates normative theory. Empirical references to platforms and political movements are illustrative. This paper belongs functionally to a special-issue introduction cluster, serving as a dialogic companion to the other public sphere papers in PSC 2024 Vol.50(1).

normative_stake.domain: public sphere / capitalism / counter-hegemony / class politics

confidence_notes: This is a dialogic/interview paper functioning as a theoretical statement. The genre is unusual: a multi-page editorial framing plus Q&A. The framing essay is normative argument by Kempf and Sevignani; Fraser's answers constitute the theoretical content. Epistemic mode: normative_with_qualitative_empirical (illustrative references to empirical phenomena). Move_sequence is compressed due to interview format — no separate rival_mapping section but Fraser engages rivals throughout. Special issue companion paper to brunkhorst et al., fischer-jarren, nosthoff-maschewski, kempf-2023, trenz-2023.
